content.text: Hybrid cloud is a deployment model in which on-premises infrastructure and one or more public cloud environments operate together as a single, integrated platform. Workloads run in whichever environment best suits their requirements — latency, compliance, cost, or burst capacity — and data flows between the environments over secure, dedicated connectivity. For most enterprises, hybrid cloud is not a temporary stepping stone to full cloud migration; it is the permanent target architecture, enabling the use of legacy on-premises systems alongside cloud-native services without forcing a choice between them.
What Hybrid Cloud Is (and Is Not)

A hybrid cloud is distinguished from simply "having some servers and also using the cloud" by the presence of orchestration and workload portability between the two environments. True hybrid cloud includes:
Unified networking.
On-premises systems can reach AWS resources using private IP addresses (not public internet). AWS resources can initiate connections back to on-premises systems.
Unified identity.

A user's Active Directory identity is recognized in both environments — either via directory synchronization (AWS IAM Identity Center + AD Connector) or federation (SAML 2.0 / OIDC). No separate cloud credentials to manage.
Unified monitoring.

A single operations view covers both environments. CloudWatch can ingest metrics from on-premises servers via the CloudWatch agent; AWS Systems Manager manages both EC2 instances and on-premises servers enrolled as managed nodes.
Workload portability.
Containerized or VM-based workloads can run identically on-premises and in the cloud, enabling migration, cloud bursting, and disaster recovery without application rewriting.
Hybrid cloud is
not

the same as multi-cloud (using multiple public clouds without on-premises) or simply running some applications on-premises and others in the cloud without connectivity between them. The defining characteristic is the secure, managed link that makes both environments behave as one.
AWS Hybrid Cloud Architecture

attrs.alt: Hybrid cloud architecture showing on-premises data center connected to AWS via Direct Connect and Site-to-Site VPN through Transit Gateway, with shared storage and identity services
attrs.title: AWS Hybrid Cloud — On-Premises + Public Cloud

content.text: The connectivity layer is the backbone of any hybrid architecture. AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated fiber link from your data center (or colocation facility) to an AWS Direct Connect location, bypassing the public internet entirely. Site-to-Site VPN provides encrypted IPSec tunnels over the internet as a backup path or primary path for smaller bandwidth requirements. Transit Gateway aggregates both connection types and routes traffic to the correct VPC.
AWS Direct Connect

AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated physical connection between your network and an AWS Direct Connect location (a colocation facility where AWS maintains a presence). The connection is a cross-connect from your cage or your network provider's port to an AWS-provided router. Traffic on the Direct Connect link never touches the public internet.
Virtual Interfaces (VIFs)
A Direct Connect connection is divided into Virtual Interfaces (VIFs), each a BGP peering session over an 802.1Q VLAN:
Private VIF.
Connects to a Virtual Private Gateway (VGW) or Transit Gateway. Traffic reaches private IP resources inside your VPCs. This is the most common VIF type for hybrid workloads.
Public VIF.
Reaches AWS public service endpoints (S3, DynamoDB, EC2 API) using public IP space, but over the dedicated fiber rather than the internet. Useful for large S3 transfers without internet egress costs.
Transit VIF.
Connects to a Direct Connect Gateway, which in turn attaches to a Transit Gateway in up to three AWS Regions. One Direct Connect connection can reach VPCs in multiple regions via Transit Gateway.

# Create a Transit Gateway for hybrid routing
TGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-transit-gateway \
  --description "Hybrid connectivity hub" \
  --options \
    AmazonSideAsn=64512,\
    AutoAcceptSharedAttachments=disable,\
    DefaultRouteTableAssociation=enable,\
    DefaultRouteTablePropagation=enable,\
    VpnEcmpSupport=enable,\
    DnsSupport=enable \
  --query 'TransitGateway.TransitGatewayId' --output text)

echo "Transit Gateway: $TGW_ID"

# Attach a VPC to the Transit Gateway
aws ec2 create-transit-gateway-vpc-attachment \
  --transit-gateway-id "$TGW_ID" \
  --vpc-id vpc-0a1b2c3d \
  --subnet-ids subnet-aza subnet-azb \
  --options DnsSupport=enable,Ipv6Support=disable

# Create a Site-to-Site VPN connection (backup path while Direct Connect is provisioned)
CGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-customer-gateway \
  --type ipsec.1 \
  --bgp-asn 65000 \
  --ip-address 203.0.113.1 \
  --query 'CustomerGateway.CustomerGatewayId' --output text)

aws ec2 create-vpn-connection \
  --type ipsec.1 \
  --customer-gateway-id "$CGW_ID" \
  --transit-gateway-id "$TGW_ID" \
  --options TunnelOptions="[{TunnelInsideCidr:'169.254.10.0/30'},{TunnelInsideCidr:'169.254.11.0/30'}]"

AWS recommends running Direct Connect with a Site-to-Site VPN as a failover. Both connect to the same Transit Gateway; BGP routing preferences (setting a higher Local Preference on Direct Connect routes) ensure that Direct Connect carries traffic by default, while the VPN automatically becomes active if Direct Connect goes down.
Hybrid DNS Resolution
In a hybrid environment, both on-premises and cloud resources need to resolve each other's DNS names. Without hybrid DNS, EC2 instances cannot resolve
db.corp.example.com
(an on-premises hostname), and on-premises servers cannot resolve
api.internal.aws.example.com
(a VPC-private hostname). Route 53 Resolver endpoints solve this:
Inbound Resolver endpoint.
One or more ENIs in your VPC that accept DNS queries from on-premises. Your on-premises DNS forwards queries for AWS-hosted zones (e.g.,
*.internal.example.com
) to this endpoint IP. Route 53 Resolver answers them.
Outbound Resolver endpoint.
Forwarding rules send DNS queries from EC2 instances for on-premises domains (e.g.,
*.corp.example.com
) to your on-premises DNS server IPs. This traverses the Direct Connect or VPN link.

# Create an Outbound Resolver endpoint for forwarding on-prem DNS queries
ENDPOINT_ID=$(aws route53resolver create-resolver-endpoint \
  --creator-request-id hybrid-outbound-$(date +%s) \
  --name hybrid-outbound \
  --security-group-ids sg-resolver \
  --direction OUTBOUND \
  --ip-addresses \
    SubnetId=subnet-aza,Ip=10.0.1.50 \
    SubnetId=subnet-azb,Ip=10.0.2.50 \
  --query 'ResolverEndpoint.Id' --output text)

# Forward corp.example.com queries to on-premises DNS servers
aws route53resolver create-resolver-rule \
  --creator-request-id rule-corp-$(date +%s) \
  --name forward-corp \
  --rule-type FORWARD \
  --domain-name corp.example.com \
  --resolver-endpoint-id "$ENDPOINT_ID" \
  --target-ips \
    Ip=192.168.1.10,Port=53 \
    Ip=192.168.1.11,Port=53
AWS Storage Gateway

AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid storage service that connects on-premises environments to AWS cloud storage. It runs as a virtual machine or hardware appliance in your data center and exposes standard storage protocols (NFS, SMB, iSCSI) to on-premises applications, while transparently tiering data to S3, S3 Glacier, or EBS in the cloud.
Gateway type
Protocol
AWS target
Primary use case
S3 File Gateway
NFS v3/v4, SMB
S3 (Standard, IA, Intelligent-Tiering)
File shares accessed from on-premises and from AWS (Lambda, EC2, SageMaker)
FSx File Gateway
SMB, NFS
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
Windows file shares with local cache, Active Directory integration
Volume Gateway
iSCSI
S3 / EBS snapshots
Block storage with cloud-backed snapshots for backup and disaster recovery
Tape Gateway
iSCSI VTL
S3 Glacier / Glacier Deep Archive
Replacing physical tape backup infrastructure with virtual tapes in the cloud
AWS Systems Manager for Hybrid Operations

AWS Systems Manager (SSM) extends AWS's fleet management capabilities to on-premises servers and VMs. By installing the SSM agent on on-premises Linux or Windows hosts and registering them as managed nodes in Systems Manager, you get a unified operations plane across your entire fleet:
Patch Manager.
Apply OS patches and application updates across EC2 instances and on-premises servers from a single patch baseline definition. Maintenance windows schedule patching during low-traffic periods.
Session Manager.

Start interactive shell sessions to on-premises servers through SSM without opening inbound SSH/RDP ports. All sessions are logged to CloudWatch Logs or S3, satisfying audit requirements for privileged access.
Inventory.
Collects hardware and software inventory from all managed nodes — EC2 and on-premises — into a central view. Useful for software license auditing and asset management across the hybrid estate.
Run Command.
Execute shell scripts or PowerShell commands across large fleets simultaneously. Run Command targets can mix EC2 instances and on-premises servers in the same operation.

# Register an on-premises server as an SSM managed node
# Step 1: Create a managed instance activation
ACTIVATION=$(aws ssm create-activation \
  --iam-role SSMServiceRole \
  --registration-limit 10 \
  --description "On-premises servers in Corp DC" \
  --output json)

ACTIVATION_ID=$(echo $ACTIVATION | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d['ActivationId'])")
ACTIVATION_CODE=$(echo $ACTIVATION | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d['ActivationCode'])")

echo "Run on the on-premises server:"
echo "sudo amazon-ssm-agent -register -id $ACTIVATION_ID -code $ACTIVATION_CODE -region us-east-1"

# After registration, run a command on all on-premises managed nodes
aws ssm send-command \
  --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
  --targets '[{"Key":"ssm:resourceType","Values":["ManagedInstance"]}]' \
  --parameters commands='["df -h","free -m","uptime"]' \
  --comment "Health check — on-premises fleet" \
  --output text
Common Hybrid Cloud Patterns
Cloud Bursting

Run workloads on-premises during normal load and automatically scale into the cloud during peaks. An Auto Scaling group with mixed instances — some launched in cloud VPC subnets when on-premises capacity is exhausted — handles burst traffic. This pattern works best for stateless web and batch-processing tiers where session state is not stored locally.
Cloud-Based Disaster Recovery

AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) continuously replicates on-premises servers to EC2 instances in a staging area. RDS snapshots and S3 CRR (Cross-Region Replication) handle databases and object storage. If the on-premises site goes down, a failover launches EC2 instances from the latest replicated disk state in minutes. The cloud DR environment costs a fraction of a full hot standby because staging instances are small; production-sized instances spin up only during actual failover.
Dev/Test in Cloud, Production On-Premises

Organizations with compliance requirements for production data residency often run their production databases on-premises or on Outposts but spin up development and test environments entirely in the public cloud. This gives developers cloud agility (instant environment provisioning, managed databases) while production data never touches the public cloud. A deployment pipeline commits artifacts to ECR, runs integration tests in a cloud VPC, and promotes to on-premises production via a deployment agent.
Data Processing at the Edge, Storage in Cloud

Manufacturing and industrial IoT workloads generate sensor data at rates too high to stream directly to the cloud. A hybrid pattern pre-processes raw sensor data on-premises (filtering, aggregation, anomaly detection) using EC2 on Outposts or on-premises Kubernetes, then sends only the processed results to S3 or Kinesis in the cloud for further analytics. The hot path stays local; the analytics and ML training happen in the cloud where compute is elastic.
Benefits of Hybrid Cloud
Preserve existing investments.

Legacy applications that cannot be easily re-architected — mainframes, specialized databases, proprietary hardware-attached software — continue running on-premises without requiring immediate migration.
Regulatory flexibility.

Keep sensitive data on-premises where regulations require, while using cloud services for less-sensitive workloads. Compliance mandates often permit cloud use with appropriate controls — hybrid lets you apply those controls at the right layer.
Gradual migration path.

Move workloads incrementally over months or years, validating each step in production without a risky big-bang cutover. Applications can be re-platformed or re-architected as part of the migration rather than simply lifted-and-shifted.
Cost optimization.

Utilize already-depreciated on-premises hardware for stable baseline workloads while using pay-per-use cloud for unpredictable or peak demand. The on-premises hardware's stranded capacity is effectively free until end-of-life.
Cloud-native innovation.
New applications and features are built cloud-native from the start, benefiting from managed services and rapid iteration, while legacy systems remain stable on-premises.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Operational complexity.

Operating two environments requires teams skilled in both on-premises infrastructure and cloud services. Incident response must span both planes — a latency issue could be in the application, the on-premises network, the Direct Connect circuit, or the cloud VPC.
Connectivity cost and latency.

Direct Connect is not free. A 1 Gbps dedicated connection costs roughly $200–400/month in port fees plus the cross-connect at the colocation facility plus data transfer charges. A 10 Gbps connection scales accordingly. And even Direct Connect introduces 5–20ms latency for inter-region traffic.
Data gravity.

Large datasets that live on-premises are expensive and slow to copy to the cloud. Applications built around that data have a strong gravity toward the on-premises environment, limiting the value of cloud migration until the data itself moves.
Security surface.

Hybrid connectivity opens a persistent, bidirectional path between your on-premises network and cloud resources. A compromised on-premises system can potentially reach cloud resources and vice versa. Segmenting the Transit Gateway route tables — allowing only specific on-premises subnets to reach specific VPC subnets — limits lateral movement.
Perpetual hybrid risk.

Organizations that intend hybrid as a migration stepping stone sometimes find the migration never completes. Legacy systems accrete dependencies; teams become comfortable with the dual-environment complexity; and the planned migration deadline slides indefinitely. Without explicit migration milestones and executive ownership, hybrid becomes permanent by default.
When to Choose Hybrid Cloud
You have significant on-premises infrastructure investment with years of remaining useful life that cannot be written off.
Regulations require some data to remain on-premises while other workloads can benefit from cloud scalability.
You need a DR target in the cloud without migrating production, or a cloud staging environment for an on-premises production system.
Latency requirements for some applications prohibit a region round-trip, while other applications are latency-tolerant and benefit from cloud elasticity.
You are executing a multi-year cloud migration and need both environments to coexist during the transition.
Connectivity Option Comparison
Choosing the right connectivity option depends on bandwidth, latency sensitivity, cost tolerance, and whether you need guaranteed throughput. The table below compares the three primary mechanisms.
Criterion
AWS Direct Connect
Site-to-Site VPN
Transit VIF via TGW
Bandwidth
1 / 10 / 100 Gbps dedicated fiber
Up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel (ECMP for more)
Same as DX port speed, multi-VPC via TGW
Latency
Consistent, sub-ms to AWS region PoP
Variable — traverses public internet
Same as DX; TGW adds ~1 ms hop
Encryption
Not encrypted by default; add MACsec or IPSec overlay
IPSec encrypted end-to-end
MACsec on DX port; IPSec on VPN attachments
Cost model
Port-hour + data transfer (lower $/GB than internet)
$0.05/hr per connection + data transfer
TGW attachment $0.05/hr + per-GB processing
Best for
Production workloads, large data volumes, low-latency apps
Backup path, DR standby, dev environments
Multi-VPC hybrid routing with single on-prem BGP peer
Provisioning a Transit Gateway Hybrid Connection

A Transit Gateway becomes the single attachment point for all your VPCs and the on-premises network. You create VPC attachments, a Direct Connect Gateway (DXGW) association, and optionally a Site-to-Site VPN as a redundant path — all sharing one route table.
Create the Transit Gateway and note its ID.
Attach each VPC to the TGW; enable route propagation in each VPC route table.
Order a Direct Connect connection, create a Transit VIF, and associate it with a Direct Connect Gateway.
Associate the DXGW with the TGW, specifying the allowed on-premises CIDR prefixes.
Create a redundant Site-to-Site VPN attached to the same TGW for automatic failover.

# 1. Create TGW
TGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-transit-gateway \
  --description "Hybrid hub" \
  --options AmazonSideAsn=64512,AutoAcceptSharedAttachments=enable,DefaultRouteTableAssociation=enable,DefaultRouteTablePropagation=enable \
  --query 'TransitGateway.TransitGatewayId' --output text)

# 2. Attach a VPC
aws ec2 create-transit-gateway-vpc-attachment \
  --transit-gateway-id "$TGW_ID" \
  --vpc-id vpc-0a1b2c3d \
  --subnet-ids subnet-0001 subnet-0002

# 3. Create a Direct Connect Gateway and associate with TGW
DXGW_ID=$(aws directconnect create-direct-connect-gateway \
  --direct-connect-gateway-name hybrid-dxgw \
  --amazon-side-asn 64513 \
  --query 'directConnectGateway.directConnectGatewayId' --output text)

aws directconnect create-direct-connect-gateway-association \
  --direct-connect-gateway-id "$DXGW_ID" \
  --gateway-id "$TGW_ID" \
  --add-allowed-prefixes-to-direct-connect-gateway '[{"cidr":"10.100.0.0/16"}]'

# 4. Add a VPN as redundant path
CGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-customer-gateway \
  --type ipsec.1 --bgp-asn 65000 --public-ip 203.0.113.1 \
  --query 'CustomerGateway.CustomerGatewayId' --output text)

aws ec2 create-vpn-connection \
  --type ipsec.1 \
  --customer-gateway-id "$CGW_ID" \
  --transit-gateway-id "$TGW_ID" \
  --options '{"StaticRoutesOnly":false}'  # BGP for automatic failover

With BGP enabled on both the Direct Connect Transit VIF and the VPN, the TGW automatically prefers the Direct Connect path (higher local preference) and fails over to VPN within seconds if the primary path goes down.
Hybrid Identity Federation
Hybrid cloud requires users and services to authenticate seamlessly across both environments. AWS offers three complementary mechanisms depending on your existing identity infrastructure.
IAM Identity Center (SSO)
— centralizes access to multiple AWS accounts and SAML 2.0 applications. Integrates with on-premises Active Directory via an AD Connector or by syncing with Microsoft Entra ID over SCIM.
AD Connector
— a directory proxy that forwards authentication requests to your on-premises Active Directory, so AD credentials unlock AWS Console and CLI access without storing any data in AWS.
AWS Managed Microsoft AD

— a fully managed AD in AWS joined to your on-premises forest via a forest or external trust. Lets AWS resources (WorkSpaces, RDS for SQL Server, FSx for Windows) join the same domain as on-premises machines.
SAML federation for CLI/SDK
— users authenticate with the on-premises IdP, receive a SAML assertion, and call
sts:AssumeRoleWithSAML
to get temporary IAM credentials. No long-lived access keys are distributed.

# Create an AD Connector pointing at on-premises Active Directory
aws ds create-ad-connector \
  --name corp.example.com \
  --short-name CORP \
  --password 'SecurePassword1!' \
  --description "Proxy to on-prem AD" \
  --size Small \
  --connect-settings '{"VpcId":"vpc-0a1b2c3d","SubnetIds":["subnet-1111","subnet-2222"],"CustomerDnsIps":["10.0.1.5","10.0.1.6"],"CustomerUserName":"svc-aws-ds"}'

# Enable IAM Identity Center once the connector is active
# (done in the console or via SSO APIs; the connector becomes the identity source)

Once the AD Connector is active, assign permission sets in IAM Identity Center. Users log in with their corporate credentials and get scoped, time-limited access to the AWS accounts they are authorized for.
Monitoring Hybrid Environments

Unified observability across on-premises and cloud requires pushing metrics and logs from both environments into a common plane. AWS provides managed agents and integrations that work across the hybrid boundary.
CloudWatch Agent on-premises

— installed on any Linux or Windows server (including SSM-managed instances) to push CPU, memory, disk, and custom metrics to CloudWatch. On-premises servers appear alongside EC2 instances in the same dashboards.
VPC Flow Logs
— capture accept/reject records for all traffic crossing the VPC. Use Athena queries on Flow Logs stored in S3 to audit which on-premises IPs are reaching which EC2 instances.
AWS Network Manager
— provides a global view of your Transit Gateway network, including on-premises BGP peers and their reachability status. Generates topology diagrams and route-change alerts.
CloudTrail

— records every AWS API call regardless of whether the caller is in the cloud or on-premises (e.g., an on-premises application calling S3 or an SSM Run Command invoked from the data center). Essential for audit trails in regulated hybrid deployments.

# Install CloudWatch agent on an SSM-managed on-premises server
aws ssm send-command \
  --document-name "AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage" \
  --parameters '{"action":["Install"],"name":["AmazonCloudWatchAgent"]}' \
  --targets '[{"Key":"tag:Environment","Values":["on-premises"]}]' \
  --region us-east-1

# After installation, start the agent with a config stored in SSM Parameter Store
aws ssm send-command \
  --document-name "AmazonCloudWatch-ManageAgent" \
  --parameters '{"action":["configure"],"mode":["ec2"],"optionalConfigurationSource":["ssm"],"optionalConfigurationLocation":["/cloudwatch-config/on-prem"]}' \
  --targets '[{"Key":"tag:Environment","Values":["on-premises"]}]'
DataSync for Bulk and Ongoing Transfers

AWS DataSync is a managed data-transfer service optimized for large-scale migrations and recurring syncs between on-premises storage and AWS storage services. It saturates available bandwidth, verifies data integrity with checksums, and transfers only changed files on subsequent runs.
Deploy the DataSync agent VM or container on-premises, then activate it in the console to link it to your AWS account.
Create a source location: NFS share, SMB share, HDFS cluster, or object storage.
Create a destination location: S3 bucket, EFS file system, FSx for Windows/Lustre/NetApp.
Create a task with a schedule, bandwidth throttle, and data-verification mode, then start it.

# Activate agent (run after deploying agent VM and opening port 443 to DataSync endpoints)
AGENT_ARN=$(aws datasync create-agent \
  --activation-key "AAAAA-BBBBB-CCCCC-DDDDD-EEEEE" \
  --agent-name on-prem-nfs-agent \
  --query 'AgentArn' --output text)

# Create source location (on-premises NFS)
SRC=$(aws datasync create-location-nfs \
  --server-hostname 10.0.1.20 \
  --subdirectory /exports/data \
  --on-prem-config AgentArns="$AGENT_ARN" \
  --query 'LocationArn' --output text)

# Create destination location (S3)
DST=$(aws datasync create-location-s3 \
  --s3-bucket-arn arn:aws:s3:::my-migration-bucket \
  --s3-storage-class STANDARD \
  --s3-config BucketAccessRoleArn=arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/DataSyncS3Role \
  --query 'LocationArn' --output text)

# Create and start the task with bandwidth throttle (100 Mbps)
TASK_ARN=$(aws datasync create-task \
  --source-location-arn "$SRC" \
  --destination-location-arn "$DST" \
  --name nfs-to-s3-sync \
  --options VerifyMode=ONLY_FILES_TRANSFERRED,BytesPerSecond=12500000 \
  --query 'TaskArn' --output text)

aws datasync start-task-execution --task-arn "$TASK_ARN"

DataSync compresses data in flight, uses multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize throughput, and verifies every transferred file against a checksum. Subsequent runs skip unchanged files, making it efficient for ongoing sync of archival data.
Network Security at the Hybrid Boundary
Extending the network boundary to on-premises increases attack surface. A layered security model — enforced at both the network and API layers — limits the blast radius of any single compromise.
Security Groups as default-deny

— attach Security Groups to EC2 and RDS instances in private subnets, allowing inbound traffic only from the specific on-premises CIDR blocks that need access. Security Groups are stateful and are the first line of defense.
Network ACLs for subnet-level filtering
— stateless rules applied to entire subnets. Use NACLs to block entire CIDR ranges known to be unauthorized and as a secondary control if Security Groups are misconfigured.
Inspection VPC with AWS Network Firewall

— route all East-West traffic (on-premises ↔ VPC) through a dedicated inspection VPC containing AWS Network Firewall. Firewall rules can perform deep-packet inspection, IDS/IPS, and domain-based filtering.
AWS GuardDuty for threat detection
— analyzes VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and CloudTrail events for anomalies. The "Hybrid" GuardDuty findings alert on unexpected lateral movement from on-premises IPs into sensitive AWS resources.

A common mistake is treating the Direct Connect or VPN link as an implicit trust boundary. Route on-premises traffic through an inspection layer before it reaches production subnets, even over a private link. An on-premises machine can be compromised, and unrestricted access to the VPC from that machine would let lateral movement propagate freely.
Key Takeaways
Hybrid cloud is defined by secure, managed connectivity and unified identity between on-premises and cloud environments — not just by using both.
AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated fiber connectivity; Transit Gateway aggregates it with VPN and multiple VPCs into a scalable routing hub.
Storage Gateway, DataSync, and Systems Manager extend cloud operational capabilities (storage tiering, data transfer, patching) to on-premises environments without requiring application changes.
Common patterns — cloud bursting, cloud-based DR, dev/test in cloud — let organizations capture cloud value incrementally while preserving on-premises investments.

Hybrid cloud adds real operational complexity and connectivity cost. It is best understood as a deliberate long-term architecture for organizations with specific constraints — not as the easiest path to the cloud.
